content:
Artificial Intelligence

Software Engineering

Future Of Work

Cybersecurity

9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Gartner has given AI-generated technical debt a name. A study of 302,600 commits has given it a number. Here is what both actually say, and what is still speculation.
A pattern has taken hold in engineering leadership circles this year: the fastest-growing job in software may turn out to be the one nobody explicitly budgeted for, cleaning up after the AI that wrote the original code. Somewhere between the first wave of AI coding tools and the second, the story quietly flipped. The pitch used to be that artificial intelligence would make software cheaper to build. Increasingly, the pitch is that someone will need to be paid, handsomely, to fix what it built.
This is not a fringe observation. It is now a documented, measured phenomenon, and it is going to reshape how organisations think about engineering governance over the next several years.

The Productivity Illusion
The first wave of enterprise AI adoption in software engineering was genuinely transformative. Copilot, Cursor, Claude, Gemini, and a wave of autonomous coding agents such as Devin allowed engineering teams to draft, refactor, and ship code at a pace that would have been unthinkable five years ago. Velocity became the metric that mattered. Pull requests per engineer went up. Time to first commit went down. Boards were shown adoption charts and told this was transformation.
It was not transformation so much as acceleration, and the two are not the same thing, because an organisation that writes more code, faster, without a matching increase in the rigour applied to reviewing, testing, and maintaining that code, is not becoming more capable, only busier. What has actually happened is that the bottleneck has moved rather than disappeared: it has shifted away from the writing of code, where AI has delivered genuine and measurable gains, and towards the checking of it, where human review capacity, security scanning, and testing infrastructure remain exactly as constrained as they were before AI arrived, with the result that all the speed gained at the point of writing simply accumulates, unspent, at the point of verification. The debt this creates was always going to come due, and the only real uncertainty was how large it would grow before an organisation was forced to notice it.

Gartner Names the Problem: AI Debt
Gartner has now named it. The firm has introduced AI Debt as a distinct category of organisational risk, defined as the accumulated cost of decisions, intentional or not, that favour short-term gains in AI-related work over long-term sustainability. The consequence is rework, inefficiency, risk, and missed opportunity, compounding over time.
Gartner’s framing is broader than code quality alone. It spans seven dimensions: strategy, value, organisation, people and culture, governance, engineering, and data. But the engineering dimension is where the debt is currently most visible and most measurable, because code either compiles or it does not, either passes a security scan or it does not, and either survives in production or it gets quietly patched out. Gartner’s central argument is that AI debt compounds faster than the technical debt organisations are used to managing, because the systems generating it evolve faster than the governance structures built to contain them. Organisations that manage this deliberately, Gartner argues, mature substantially faster than those that do not. Organisations that ignore it risk stalled projects and wasted investment.
This is the part of that pattern that holds up under scrutiny. Gartner has not merely commented on AI-generated technical debt. It has built a framework around it, on the premise that it needs to be detected, prioritised, and remediated as its own discipline, not folded into the general technical debt backlog and forgotten.

The Evidence: Inside 302,600 Commits
Frameworks are one thing. Numbers are another, and this is where the claim moves from plausible to well evidenced.
A large-scale empirical study, “Debt Behind the AI Boom: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of AI-Generated Code in the Wild,” analysed 302,600 verified AI-authored commits across 6,299 GitHub repositories. The study covered five of the most widely used AI coding assistants: GitHub Copilot, Claude, Cursor, Gemini, and Devin. Commits were identified through explicit Git metadata signals and then run through static analysis tooling, including ESLint, Pylint, and Semgrep, before and after each commit, to attribute quality issues directly to the AI-authored change. The researchers report ninety-nine per cent attribution precision and 85.9 per cent precision in issue detection, which is a rigorous methodology for a study of this scale.
The headline figure is stark. Across those commits, the study identified 484,366 distinct issues, just under half a million, and more than fifteen per cent of commits from every single AI coding assistant tested introduced at least one issue. This was not confined to one vendor or one model family. It was consistent across the board.
More striking still is what happened to those issues afterwards. Roughly 22.7 per cent, close to a quarter, were still present in the latest version of the affected repository at the time of the study. These were not caught in review, not flagged in a linter run, not quietly reverted. They shipped, and they stayed shipped.
Corroborating evidence exists elsewhere. A separate analysis by CodeRabbit, reviewing 470 open-source pull requests using a structured issue taxonomy, found that AI-generated code produced roughly 1.7 times more issues overall than comparable human-written code, though the gap varied sharply by category: readability issues were roughly three times more common, security issues up to 2.7 times more common, and logic and correctness issues around 1.75 times more common. The sample size is smaller and the methodology different, but the direction of the finding is the same: AI-generated code is not simply faster to produce, it is measurably less reliable on first pass than the code it is replacing.

Where the Debt Actually Hides
Not all of that debt looks the same, and the composition matters for anyone deciding where to point remediation effort.
Code Smells (89.3 per cent of issues). The overwhelming majority of issues were not outright bugs but structural weaknesses: duplicated logic, poor naming, excessive complexity, and patterns that make future modification harder and riskier. This is unglamorous, low-drama debt. It rarely causes an incident on its own. It does, however, compound, and it is exactly the category most likely to be waved through in a review focused on “does it work” rather than “is it maintainable.”
Correctness Issues (6.0 per cent of issues). A smaller but far more consequential category: logic errors, incorrect edge-case handling, and outright bugs. These are the issues most likely to surface as customer-visible defects, and the ones most expensive to trace back to their origin once they have been sitting in production for months.
Security Issues (4.7 per cent of issues). The smallest category by volume but the highest by consequence. Nearly twenty-three thousand distinct security issues were introduced across the dataset, into production codebases, by AI coding assistants that were, in every case, doing exactly what they were asked to do: write code that worked.
This is the pattern organisations need to internalise. AI-generated technical debt is not primarily a story about AI writing broken code. Most of what it writes runs. The debt is structural and cumulative, sitting quietly in the 89.3 per cent of issues that look like nothing until they are everything, at exactly the moment an organisation tries to move fast on a codebase that can no longer support it.

The Governance Gap Nobody Budgeted For
Ask most engineering leaders how AI-generated code is reviewed, and the honest answer is: the same way human-written code is reviewed, by the same reviewers, on the same timelines, at a volume those reviewers were never resourced for. This is the gap the numbers above are actually describing. A fifteen per cent issue-introduction rate is not a crisis if the code volume is small and the review capacity is generous. It becomes a structural problem the moment code volume triples and review headcount does not, which is precisely the situation most organisations that have adopted AI coding assistants now find themselves in.
This is a governance failure, not a tooling failure, and it maps directly onto a gap in the Agentic Readiness Model. Organisations have invested heavily in the Technology pillar, buying and deploying coding assistants at pace, while under-investing in the Governance pillar that should sit alongside it. The result is an organisation that has substituted human typing speed for AI typing speed, without redesigning the review, testing, and accountability structures that were built for the slower, human-paced world. Debt is not a defect in the model. It is the predictable output of deploying acceleration without redesigning the checks that were calibrated for a slower system.

The Cleanup Economy Being Built Right Now
This is where that pattern stops being anecdotal and becomes a genuine market signal. If AI is generating verifiable, quantifiable, persistent technical debt at scale, then somebody is going to be paid to remediate it, and three categories of business are already positioning to be that somebody.
Consultancies are building AI code auditing and remediation practices, distinct from traditional technical debt advisory, because the failure modes are different enough to require a different diagnostic approach. Cybersecurity firms are treating AI-generated vulnerabilities as a growth segment in their own right, given that nearly five per cent of the issues in the study were security-relevant and introduced at a pace no manual review process was built to absorb. Enterprise software vendors are racing to build governance and static analysis tooling purpose-built for AI-authored code, on the premise that the tools which created the problem at scale are not the same tools needed to catch it at scale.
None of this proves the specific claim that AI-generated technical debt will become a trillion-dollar industry. That figure did not originate in Gartner’s research or in the commit study. It is an extrapolation built on top of solid evidence, and it should be read as exactly that: a plausible prediction, not a verified fact in its own right. What the evidence does establish, cleanly, is the underlying mechanism. AI is writing code faster than most organisations can review it, a measurable share of that code carries structural and security debt, and a meaningful proportion of it survives, undetected, into production. Wherever there is a large, growing, and currently under-governed liability, a market forms to manage it. That much is not speculation.

Where to Start
The organisations that will avoid becoming case studies in this trend are the ones treating AI-generated code as labour output requiring governance, not as software output requiring adoption metrics. Three steps matter most in the next twelve months.

• Treat AI-authored commits as a distinct category in code review, with static analysis and security scanning applied before merge, not after an incident.

• Track a debt-survival metric, the share of AI-introduced issues still present after a fixed window, rather than only tracking commit volume or velocity.

• Assign explicit ownership for AI code governance to a named role, rather than leaving it to fall between engineering leadership and security, where it currently tends to sit unattended.
This points to Mehtadology’s Puppetmaster Competency: not a new discipline bolted onto an existing role, but a single human role that absorbs
